items.subtopic: Gait Disorders, Imbalance, and Falls | PREVALENCE, MORBIDITY, AND MORTALITY
items.title: Prevalence of Gait Disorders in the Elderly
items.content: Gait disorders are prevalent among older adults, significantly impacting their risk for falls. These disorders affect 15% of individuals over age 65 and nearly 40% of those aged 85 or older.
keyPoints: 15% prevalence of gait disorders in individuals >65 years old
One person in four uses a mechanical aid by age 80
Approximately 40% prevalence among those ≥85 years old
Gait disorders are major risk factors for falls and injury
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · Gait Disorders, Imbalance, and Falls | PREVALENCE, MORBIDITY, AND MORTALITY | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Gait Disorders, Imbalance, and Falls | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Anatomy and Physiology of Gait and Balance Control
items.content: Bipedal gait relies on the integration of postural control and locomotion, involving multiple central nervous system regions. Sensory information from visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive systems is crucial for maintaining balance.
keyPoints: Integration of postural control and locomotion is essential for upright bipedal gait
Long latency responses in leg muscles begin 110 milliseconds after perturbation
Balance relies on sensory inputs from visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive systems
Loss of two out of three sensory pathways can compromise balance
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · Gait Disorders, Imbalance, and Falls | PREVALENCE, MORBIDITY, AND MORTALITY | ANATOMY AND PHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF GAIT | CAUTIOUS GAIT
items.title: Understanding Cautious Gait: Adaptation to Perceived Postural Threat
items.content: Cautious gait is characterized by an abbreviated stride, widened base, and lowered center of mass. This adaptation occurs in response to a perceived threat to balance stability, often accompanied by fear of falling.
keyPoints: Cautious gait involves walking with an abbreviated stride, widened base, and lowered center of mass.
Arms are often held abducted during cautious gait.
This gait pattern is common in older patients with gait impairment, observed in more than one-third of such cases.
Physical therapy can improve walking and may reveal a more specific underlying disorder upon follow-up.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · DISORDERS OF GAIT | CAUTIOUS GAIT

items.subtopic: DISORDERS OF GAIT | CAUTIOUS GAIT
items.title: Role of Physical Therapy in Cautious Gait: Uncovering Underlying Disorders
items.content: Physical therapy is effective in improving cautious gait, potentially revealing specific underlying disorders through follow-up observation.
keyPoints: Cautious gait is a common adaptation to perceived postural threats, especially in older adults.
More than one-third of older patients with gait impairment exhibit cautious gait.
Physical therapy can enhance walking ability and may help identify specific underlying disorders upon further evaluation.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · DISORDERS OF GAIT | CAUTIOUS GAIT

items.subtopic: STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS
items.title: Characteristics and Causes of Spastic Gait
items.content: Spastic gait is marked by leg stiffness, muscle tone imbalance, and toe walking or 'scissoring' due to increased adductor tone. It results from corticospinal pathway compromise and heightened spinal reflex activity. Physical examination reveals upper motor neuron signs.
keyPoints: Stiffness in legs with circumduction and scuffing of feet
Compromise of corticospinal command and overactivity of spinal reflexes
Upper motor neuron signs present on physical exam
Common causes: cervical spondylosis (elderly), demyelinating disease, trauma (younger patients)
Differential diagnosis includes dystonia and stiff-person syndrome
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS

items.subtopic: STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS
items.title: Parkinsonism: Features and Management of Freezing Gait
items.content: Freezing gait in Parkinson's disease is an episodic inability to move forward despite intent. It can be triggered by environmental factors like narrow doorways or crowds, increasing fall risk. Approximately 25% of patients experience freezing within five years of onset.
keyPoints: 1% prevalence in population >65 years
Characteristic features: stooped posture, shuffling gait, decreased arm swing
Freezing affects ~25% of Parkinson's patients within 5 years
Management includes dopamine replacement and cholinesterase inhibitors like donepezil or rivastigmine to reduce falls
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS

items.subtopic: STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS
items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Freezing Gait in Neurodegenerative Disorders
items.content: Freezing gait is not exclusive to Parkinson's disease and occurs in other neurodegenerative disorders like PSP, multiple-system atrophy, and corticobasal degeneration. These conditions often present with axial stiffness, postural instability, and a shuffling gait without the pill-rolling tremor typical of Parkinson's.
keyPoints: Freezing common in progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP), multiple-system atrophy
Differentiation: PSP has more erect posture; vascular parkinsonism shows broad-based gait with reduced arm swing bilaterally
Early disproportionate gait involvement suggests vascular parkinsonism
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS

items.subtopic: FRONTAL GAIT DISORDER
items.title: Why consider vascular disease as a primary cause of frontal gait disorder?
items.content: Frontal gait disorder, often seen in the elderly, is frequently caused by vascular disease. This condition manifests with shuffling and freezing gait due to higher cerebral dysfunction. The most common etiology involves subcortical small-vessel disease affecting deep frontal white matter and centrum ovale.
keyPoints: Vascular disease is the most common cause of frontal gait disorder.
Over three-quarters of patients with subcortical vascular dementia exhibit gait abnormalities.
Characteristic features include decreased arm swing, stooped posture, dysarthria, pseudobulbar affect, increased tone, and hyperreflexia in lower limbs.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · FRONTAL GAIT DISORDER

items.subtopic: FRONTAL GAIT DISORDER
items.title: How to differentiate normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) from other causes of frontal gait disorder?
items.content: Normal pressure hydrocephalus presents with a similar gait disorder as seen in vascular disease and neurodegenerative conditions. Diagnosis is supported by MRI findings such as ventricular enlargement, enlarged flow void about the aqueduct, periventricular white matter changes, and high-convexity tightness. Confirmation requires lumbar puncture or dynamic testing.
keyPoints: NPH presents with gait disorder similar to vascular disease and neurodegenerative dementias.
MRI findings include ventricular enlargement, enlarged flow void about the aqueduct, periventricular white matter change, and high-convexity tightness.
Lumbar puncture or dynamic test is necessary for NPH diagnosis.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · FRONTAL GAIT DISORDER

items.subtopic: CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE
items.title: Recognizing Cerebellar Gait Ataxia: Key Features and Causes
items.content: Cerebellar gait ataxia is identified by a wide base of support, lateral trunk instability, erratic foot placement, and balance decompensation on narrow bases. Early signs include difficulty turning and inability to walk heel-to-toe with truncal sway. Common causes in older patients are stroke, trauma, tumor, neurodegenerative diseases like multiple-system atrophy, hereditary cerebellar degeneration, fragile X premutation, and alcohol-related damage.
keyPoints: Wide base of support, lateral instability, erratic foot placement
Difficulty maintaining balance when turning is an early feature
Causes: stroke, trauma, tumor, neurodegenerative diseases (e.g., multiple-system atrophy), hereditary cerebellar degeneration, fragile X premutation in older men, alcohol
MRI shows extent and topography of cerebellar atrophy
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE
items.title: Distinguishing Sensory Ataxia: Clinical Presentation and Causes
items.content: Sensory ataxia results from impaired afferent information affecting balance. Classic examples include tabetic neurosyphilis, with contemporary equivalents being large-fiber neuropathy. Vitamin B12 deficiency is a treatable cause of sensory loss in the spinal cord and peripheral nervous system. Patients exhibit destabilized stance upon eye closure, often looking down at their feet while walking and struggling in darkness.
keyPoints: Impaired balance due to degraded afferent information
Classic example: tabetic neurosyphilis; contemporary equivalent: large-fiber neuropathy
Treatable cause: Vitamin B12 deficiency leading to sensory loss
Stance destabilized by eye closure, patients look down at feet when walking and do poorly in the dark
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: TOXIC AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL GAIT DISORDER | Slowly Progressive Disorder of Gait
items.title: Recognizing and Managing Toxic/Metabolic Causes of Gait Disorders
items.content: Chronic toxicity from medications such as neuroleptics and long-acting benzodiazepines, along with metabolic disturbances like chronic renal disease or hepatic failure, can impair gait by affecting postural control. These conditions often present with mental status changes, asterixis, or myoclonus, leading to an increased risk of falls. Recognizing these treatable disorders is crucial for effective management.
keyPoints: Chronic toxicity from medications (e.g., neuroleptics, long-acting benzodiazepines) affects postural control and increases fall risk.
Metabolic disturbances in chronic renal disease or hepatic failure can impair gait through asterixis affecting postural support.
Mental status changes, asterixis, myoclonus are examination findings associated with these disorders.
These conditions are often treatable.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · TOXIC AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL GAIT DISORDER | Slowly Progressive Disorder of Gait

items.subtopic: TOXIC AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL GAIT DISORDER | Slowly Progressive Disorder of Gait
items.title: Diagnosing Functional Gait Disorders: Key Features and Differentiation
items.content: Functional neurologic disorders, often presenting with gait abnormalities, are characterized by sudden onset, inconsistent deficits, waxing and waning symptoms, and improvement with distraction. These patients may exhibit extreme slow motion or foot stiffness (dystonia). Falls are rare, and there is often a discrepancy between examination findings and functional status. Differentiating these from organic causes like major depression is essential.
keyPoints: Sudden onset, inconsistent deficits, waxing and waning course are key features of functional gait disorders.
Improvement with distraction and incongruence of symptoms with an organic lesion are diagnostic clues.
Extreme slow motion, overcautious gait, astasia-abasia, foot stiffness (dystonia) may be observed.
Falls are rare in functional gait disorders; discrepancies between examination findings and functional status are common.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · TOXIC AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL GAIT DISORDER | Slowly Progressive Disorder of Gait

items.subtopic: DEFINITION, ETIOLOGY, AND MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Understanding Balance and Its Complex Mechanisms
items.content: Balance is a dynamic state of equilibrium maintained by integrating inputs from cerebellar, vestibular, and somatosensory systems. The premotor cortex and various tracts mediate the necessary motor outputs to maintain posture.
keyPoints: Balance involves maintaining equilibrium with respect to lower extremities, gravity, and support surface.
Cerebellar, vestibular, and somatosensory inputs are crucial for balance.
Motor output is mediated by the premotor cortex and corticospinal/reticulospinal tracts.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · DEFINITION, ETIOLOGY, AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION, ETIOLOGY, AND MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Differentiating Causes of Imbalance: Cerebellar Ataxia vs. Vestibular Disorders
items.content: Cerebellar ataxia presents with visible balance impairment without dizziness, while vestibular disorders manifest through vertigo, nystagmus, and impaired standing balance.
keyPoints: Cerebellar ataxia: Patients do not typically complain of dizziness but show visible balance issues.
Vestibular disorders: Symptoms include vertigo (subjective perception of movement), nystagmus, and impaired standing balance.
Not all patients with vestibular deficits exhibit vertigo or nystagmus; balance may be impaired on standing and walking.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · DEFINITION, ETIOLOGY, AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: FALLS | RISK FACTORS FOR FALLS
items.title: Prevalence and Consequences of Falls in the Elderly
items.content: Falls are a significant concern among individuals over 65, with more than one-third experiencing falls annually. The consequences can be severe due to medical comorbidities like osteoporosis, leading to complications such as hip fractures which increase hospitalization rates and mortality risk.
keyPoints: Over one-third of people aged >65 living in the community fall each year.
Hip fractures from falls lead to increased hospitalization and a substantially higher mortality risk within the subsequent year.
Falls can result in brain or spinal injuries, with spinal cord injury rates doubling in individuals over 65 in the past decade.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · FALLS | RISK FACTORS FOR FALLS

items.subtopic: FALLS | RISK FACTORS FOR FALLS
items.title: Impact of Fear and Functional Limitations Due to Falls
items.content: The fear of falling can lead to significant lifestyle changes among the elderly, with nearly one in five restricting their activities. This restriction results in diminished quality of life and increased morbidity and mortality rates.
keyPoints: Nearly one in five elderly individuals limit their activity due to fear of falling.
Loss of ambulation from falls leads to decreased quality of life and higher morbidity and mortality.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · FALLS | RISK FACTORS FOR FALLS

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS
items.title: Identifying High-Risk Patients for Falls
items.content: Prospective identification of high-risk patients is crucial to prevent serious injuries from falls. The Timed Up and Go (TUG) test is a simple tool used to assess fall risk, with a completion time greater than 12 seconds indicating higher risk.
keyPoints: All community-dwelling adults should be asked annually about falls and fear of falling.
The TUG test involves timing the patient as they stand up from a chair, walk 10 feet, turn, and sit down.
Patients requiring >12 seconds to complete the TUG test are at high risk for falls.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS
items.title: Evaluating Fall History and Mechanisms
items.content: A detailed history of falls, including mechanisms and provoking factors, is essential to identify underlying causes. Sudden drops may indicate syncope or seizures, while falls after minor perturbations suggest muscular strength issues.
keyPoints: History surrounding a fall can be problematic; an observer's report is invaluable.
Sudden drop without provocation raises suspicion for syncope or seizure.
Falls after arising from a chair suggest muscular strength issues.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS
items.title: Physical Examination in Fall Assessment
items.content: A comprehensive physical examination, including cardiac evaluation and orthostatic blood pressure measurements, is critical. A systolic drop of >20 mmHg or a diastolic drop >10 mmHg upon standing indicates the need for further investigation.
keyPoints: Cardiac examination should look for rhythm irregularities or murmurs.
Supine blood pressure compared to standing pressures evaluates orthostasis.
A systolic drop of >20 mmHg, a diastolic drop >10 mmHg, or marked increase in heart rate warrants investigation.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS

items.question: Which feature is typically observed in patients with frontal gait disorder?

Increased arm swing and upright posture
Preserved strength but inability to make stepping movements when not standing
Normal arm swing and absence of shuffling
items.explanation: Patients with frontal gait disorder typically exhibit a wide base of support, short stride, and shuffling along the floor.

Vascular disease, particularly subcortical small-vessel disease

items.statement: Dopamine replacement therapy improves gait initiation in Parkinson's disease.
items.explanation: Dopamine replacement improves step length, arm swing, turning speed, and gait initiation.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS

items.subtopic: STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS
items.statement: Freezing of gait in Parkinson's disease is always responsive to changes in levodopa.
items.explanation: In patients with motor fluctuations, freezing occurring in the 'on' state often fails to respond to changes in levodopa.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS

items.subtopic: FRONTAL GAIT DISORDER
items.statement: Strength is generally preserved in patients with frontal gait disorder.
items.explanation: Patients are able to make stepping movements when not standing and maintaining their balance, indicating preserved strength.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · FRONTAL GAIT DISORDER

items.subtopic: FRONTAL GAIT DISORDER
items.statement: Frontal gait disorder is primarily caused by neurodegenerative dementias.
items.explanation: The most common cause of frontal gait disorder is vascular disease, not neurodegenerative dementias.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · FRONTAL GAIT DISORDER

items.subtopic: CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE
items.statement: Patients with cerebellar gait ataxia can walk tandem heel to toe without difficulty.
items.explanation: Patients with cerebellar gait ataxia are unable to walk tandem heel to toe.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE
items.statement: Sensory ataxia can be caused by neuropathy affecting large fibers.
items.explanation: Neuropathy affecting large fibers is a contemporary equivalent of sensory ataxia.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: TOXIC AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL GAIT DISORDER | Slowly Progressive Disorder of Gait
items.statement: Sedative drugs, such as neuroleptics and long-acting benzodiazepines, do not affect postural control.
items.explanation: Sedative drugs like neuroleptics and long-acting benzodiazepines affect postural control and increase the risk for falls.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · TOXIC AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL GAIT DISORDER | Slowly Progressive Disorder of Gait

items.subtopic: TOXIC AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL GAIT DISORDER | Slowly Progressive Disorder of Gait
items.statement: MRI is a sensitive test for detecting cerebral lesions in patients with undiagnosed gait disorders.
items.explanation: MRI is sensitive for cerebral lesions and serves as a good screening test for occult hydrocephalus.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · TOXIC AND METABOLIC DISORDERS | FUNCTIONAL GAIT DISORDER | Slowly Progressive Disorder of Gait

items.subtopic: DEFINITION, ETIOLOGY, AND MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Patients with vestibular deficits related to ototoxic drugs often present with vertigo.
items.explanation: These patients may lack vertigo or obvious nystagmus.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · DEFINITION, ETIOLOGY, AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION, ETIOLOGY, AND MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Romberg's sign indicates compromised postural control due to somatosensory deficits.
items.explanation: Romberg's sign is used to assess postural control in the context of somatosensory deficits.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · DEFINITION, ETIOLOGY, AND MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: FALLS | RISK FACTORS FOR FALLS
items.statement: Over one-third of people aged >65 living in the community fall each year.
items.explanation: The passage states that over one-third of individuals aged >65 who live in the community experience falls annually.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · FALLS | RISK FACTORS FOR FALLS

items.subtopic: FALLS | RISK FACTORS FOR FALLS
items.statement: Hip fractures due to falls are not associated with increased mortality.
items.explanation: The passage notes that hip fractures from falls are linked to a substantially increased mortality risk.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · FALLS | RISK FACTORS FOR FALLS

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS
items.statement: A sudden drop without provocation should raise suspicion for syncope, seizure, or other neurologic events.
items.explanation: A sudden drop without provocation is indicative of potential syncope, seizure, or other neurological issues.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS
items.statement: Falls associated with loss of consciousness do not require cardiac or neurologic evaluation.
items.explanation: Falls associated with loss of consciousness may require appropriate cardiac or neurological evaluation and intervention.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS

items.subtopic: STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS
items.assertion: Freezing of gait is a common feature in progressive supranuclear palsy.
items.reason: PSP patients frequently present with axial stiffness and postural instability.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why freezing of gait is common in PSP.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS

items.subtopic: STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS
items.assertion: Dystonia often results from a genetic basis.
items.reason: Asymmetric dystonia involving gait may indicate Parkinson's disease in younger adults.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why dystonia often has a genetic basis.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · STIFF-LEGGED GAIT | PARKINSONISM, FREEZING GAIT, AND OTHER MOVEMENT DISORDERS

items.subtopic: CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE
items.assertion: Patients with neuromuscular disease often have an abnormal gait.
items.reason: Weakness of the hip girdle may result in some degree of excess pelvic sway during locomotion.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the sole explanation for the abnormal gait.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · CEREBBELLAR GAIT ATAXIA | SENSORY ATAXIA | NEUROMUSCULAR DISEASE

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS
items.assertion: Patients with a history of falls should undergo further assessment if they take medications like benzodiazepines.
items.reason: Benzodiazepines are associated with an increased risk of falls.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients taking benzodiazepines should be assessed further.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS
items.assertion: Falls occurring in changing footing situations suggest a muscular strength issue.
items.reason: Such falls indicate possible somatosensory, visual, or vestibular deficits.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these falls suggest sensory or balance issues rather than purely muscular strength problems.
items.reference: Gait Disorders Imbalance and Falls · ASSESSMENT OF THE PATIENT WITH FALLS
